questions.prompt: What does 'eventually consistent' mean for KV?

choices.text: Writes propagate to all 300+ edges within ~60s; reads serve from nearest cache (may be stale). Sub-ms reads from edge after first hit. Right for sessions, flags, configs, caches (where eventual consistency is fine). Wrong for transactional data or strict rate limits — use Durable Objects/D1 for those.

questions.explanation: Writes propagate within ~60s; reads serve from cache (may be stale).

questions.prompt: Defining cost advantage of R2 over S3?

choices.text: Zero egress fees. S3 charges $0.05-0.09/GB for data leaving AWS; at any scale this dominates costs. R2 charges $0 for egress. For external-serving workloads (images, downloads, media), the savings are massive. R2 also has slightly cheaper storage per GB. Pair with Workers Cache API for edge caching.

questions.explanation: Zero egress fees. Dominates economics for serving workloads.

choices.text: Managed SQLite database with global read replicas. Full SQL (joins, indexes, transactions, window functions). Reads serve from regional replicas (sub-ms at edge); writes go to primary region. Up to ~10GB per database; fits typical OLTP workloads. Drizzle ORM for type-safe schema/queries. For larger or PostgreSQL features, use Hyperdrive + external Postgres.

questions.explanation: Managed SQLite with global read replicas. Up to 10GB; OLTP shape.

questions.prompt: User uploads (avatars, docs) on a Workers app. Architecture?

choices.text: Split: D1 for metadata (user records, file metadata rows with id/owner_id/filename/mime_type/r2_key/size/uploaded_at), R2 for file contents at the r2_key path. Relational queries via D1; file retrieval from R2 (cheap egress). Avoids huge BLOBs in D1; gives queryable metadata R2 alone can't. Add Cache API in front of R2 for hot files. Standard architecture for any Workers app with user uploads.

questions.explanation: D1 for metadata (relational); R2 for files. Standard upload pattern.

questions.prompt: What do Durable Objects uniquely provide?

choices.text: Strong consistency via single-leader, single-threaded coordination. One DO per entity (per user, per room, per match) — each lives in one location, serializes its requests, provides linearizable operations on its state. Solves what KV/D1 can't: 'exactly one source of truth for this entity, operations must be ordered.' Use cases: exact rate limiting, chat rooms, game state, shopping carts, distributed locks. Many DOs run independently; single-threaded design caps per-DO throughput but provides guarantees nothing else does.

questions.explanation: Strong consistency via single-leader coordination. One source of truth per entity.
